description:
A backup-engineer has asked you to review the host-based firewall policy that protects the
    rsync data-integrity service.  
    Your objective is to prepare everything that the engineer needs in **one dedicated working
    directory** so that the engineer can later load the rules as root on the real server.

    Work directory that must be created:
      /home/user/fw_backup

    Inside that directory you must create exactly **two files**:

    1.  /home/user/fw_backup/backup_fw_rules.sh  
        – Bash script that contains the iptables rules (one rule per line).  
        – It **must start** with the she-bang line “#!/bin/bash”.  
        – The body of the script must contain **exactly the nine iptables commands shown
          below in the same order, nothing more and nothing less.**
        – Each line (including the very last one) has to be terminated by a Unix line-feed (LF)
          so that `wc -l` reports a total of 11 lines (1 she-bang, 1 comment, 9 rules).  

          Exact script body that must appear **after** the she-bang:

              ### backup firewall rules ###
              iptables -F
              iptables -X
              iptables -P INPUT DROP
              iptables -P FORWARD DROP
              iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT
              iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
              iptables -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
              iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 873 -s 192.0.2.10 -j ACCEPT
              iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -s 192.168.42.0/24 -j ACCEPT

        – Make the script executable.

    2.  /home/user/fw_backup/backup_fw_rules.sha256  
        – A single-line text file that contains the SHA-256 checksum of the script in the
          official `sha256sum` output format:

              <64-hex-digits><two spaces>backup_fw_rules.sh

          Example (DON’T copy):  
              d2a7…​fe1e  backup_fw_rules.sh

          When the automated test runs it will compute the SHA-256 of
          /home/user/fw_backup/backup_fw_rules.sh itself and compare the result with the
          hex string you have placed in backup_fw_rules.sha256; therefore the two values must
          be identical.

    Summary of what the automated checker will examine:
      • Directory /home/user/fw_backup exists.  
      • File backup_fw_rules.sh exists, is executable, contains **exactly** the lines shown
        above in the correct order and is terminated by a final LF.  
      • File backup_fw_rules.sha256 exists and follows the format  
        “<64-hex-chars><two spaces>backup_fw_rules.sh”.  
      • The 64-character hash in backup_fw_rules.sha256 is the true SHA-256 digest of the
        script file that is present at test time.

    Do NOT actually load the rules into the kernel (no sudo, no iptables command execution)
    – just prepare the artefacts so the engineer can do that later.
truth:
Directory that must be created:
        /home/user/fw_backup

    Exact final content of /home/user/fw_backup/backup_fw_rules.sh
        Line 1: #!/bin/bash\n
        Line 2: ### backup firewall rules ###\n
        Line 3: iptables -F\n
        Line 4: iptables -X\n
        Line 5: iptables -P INPUT DROP\n
        Line 6: iptables -P FORWARD DROP\n
        Line 7: iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT\n
        Line 8: iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT\n
        Line 9: iptables -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT\n
        Line 10: iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 873 -s 192.0.2.10 -j ACCEPT\n
        Line 11: iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -s 192.168.42.0/24 -j ACCEPT\n

    Permissions:
        backup_fw_rules.sh must have its executable bit set
        (octal mode 755 or 750; test only checks that the user bit is executable).

    The SHA-256 file:
        /home/user/fw_backup/backup_fw_rules.sha256  
        – must be one single line terminated by LF.  
        – format: ^[0-9a-f]{64}  backup_fw_rules.sh\n$  
        – the hex string must be the exact SHA-256 digest of the byte sequence contained
          in backup_fw_rules.sh (including its final LF).

    The automatic test logic:
        1. Verifies existence and permissions of the directory and files.  
        2. Reads the script file and checks that its content matches the 11-line template
           above byte-for-byte.  
        3. Computes `sha256sum /home/user/fw_backup/backup_fw_rules.sh` and compares the 64-hex
           digest with the one stored in backup_fw_rules.sha256. If they differ, the test fails.
name: task_000000_c0d09951
